findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether `baseline_shadow.py` exists (it is absent from the repo today); either remove it from the 10-site list and all AC references, or explicitly scope its creation into this story.
evidence: Directory listing of `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/` shows no `baseline_shadow.py` file.
Search for `baseline_shadow` in `src/` returns zero hits (only ADR documentation and external experiment corpora reference it).
ADR 0035 §(a) table and §(c) layout diagram reference `baseline_shadow.py` as a future/planned file in the store group, but it is not present in the repo.
findings.finding: `baseline_shadow.py` is named as one of the 10 differ/apply leak sites but does not exist as a source file in the `rebar_reconciler/` package.
findings.location: Context / Problem section, Edit-set bullet: 'classify.py, reconcile_check.py, baseline_shadow.py, outbound_links.py, binding_walk.py, reconcile.py'; also Scope and Acceptance Criteria AC#1
scenarios: The neutrality gate (AC#1) counts 10 sites; if `baseline_shadow.py` doesn't exist, the gate either errors or silently passes with only 9 sites, giving false assurance.
An implementer searching for the file to rewire will not find it, creating ambiguity about whether it must be created first or the count should be 9.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `baseline_shadow.py` from the named leak-site list (making it a 9-site story) and update AC#1 and the Scope section accordingly, since the file does not exist and no other part of the plan creates it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `apply_inbound.py` to the edit-set and the neutrality gate scope, as it directly imports `_map_local_to_jira_fields` from `outbound_fields` and is an unenumerated differ/apply leak site.

`apply_inbound.py` line 492: `fields = _map_local_to_jira_fields(ticket, binding_store=binding_store, ...)`
The plan's enumeration lists `apply_inbound_records.py` (a split-out helper) but omits the parent `apply_inbound.py` which also imports the vendor module directly.
findings.finding: `apply_inbound.py` directly imports `from rebar_reconciler.outbound_fields import _map_local_to_jira_fields` (line 49) and uses it in `_build_hard_delete_recreate`, but is not named in the 10 differ/apply leak sites, so the neutrality gate in AC#1 would pass while leaving a live vendor import untouched.
findings.location: Scope section (10 leak sites), Acceptance Criteria AC#1
scenarios: After rewiring all 10 named sites the neutrality grep-gate passes, but `apply_inbound.py` still imports `outbound_fields` directly — the core-neutrality goal is not achieved.
The `_build_hard_delete_recreate` path (hard-delete re-create follow-on) continues to call the Jira field mapper without going through the Backend port.

Grep of src/ for 'baseline_shadow.py' returns zero matches

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` does not exist (grep confirms zero hits); update the Scope and AC language from 'remove duplicated link-relation constants' to 'relocate the single copy in `outbound_links.py` behind the mapper' to accurately describe the work.
evidence: Plan scope: 'Single-source the duplicated priority/status value-maps and link-relation constants behind the mapper (remove the core copies)'
outbound_links.py:30-42 comment: 'The canonical definition lives in acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK (Cycle 1), but the differ is loaded standalone…so we re-declare a local copy here'
Grep of acli_graph.py for '_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK' returns zero matches — no such symbol exists in acli_graph.py
Grep of entire src/ for '_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK' returns only outbound_links.py — there is exactly one copy, not a duplicate
findings.finding: The plan asserts that link-relation constants are duplicated between outbound_links.py and 'the canonical definition in acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK', but acli_graph.py contains no _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK symbol — only outbound_links.py has one.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Single-source the duplicated priority/status value-maps and link-relation constants behind the mapper (remove the core copies)'
scenarios: Implementer looks for a second copy of _RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK in acli_graph to deduplicate but finds none; the 'remove the core copies' instruction for link constants is vacuous — there is nothing to remove from acli_graph.

The AC grep checking zero hits for 'link-relation constants' in core may pass trivially if the constant was never in acli_graph, masking whether the outbound_links.py copy was actually moved to the adapter.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'duplicated…link-relation constants' with 'the link-relation constant in outbound_links.py (_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK)' in Scope and AC, since only one copy exists.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the link-relation constants scope: `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` has only one copy (in `outbound_links.py`, not duplicated in `acli_graph.py`); either explicitly describe what action this story takes on it, or remove 'link-relation constants' from the deduplication claim.
evidence: Search for `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` across the entire repo returns exactly one hit: `outbound_links.py:42`.

`acli_graph.py` contains no definition or reference to `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` — the comment in `outbound_links.py` line 30 ('The canonical definition lives in acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK') is stale.
Plan states to 'remove the core copies' implying multiple exist; only one copy exists.
findings.finding: The plan asserts there are 'duplicated link-relation constants' to single-source, but `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` exists only once — in `outbound_links.py`. The comment there claims the canonical copy lives in `acli_graph._RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK (Cycle 1)`, but `acli_graph.py` contains no such constant. There is no duplication to remove; the plan's single-sourcing target for link constants is based on a stale code comment.
findings.location: Edit-set bullet: 'Single-source the duplicated priority/status value-maps and link-relation constants behind the mapper (remove the core copies)'
scenarios: Implementer searches for the duplicate to remove and finds none in `acli_graph.py`, creating confusion about what 'remove the core copies' means for link constants.
If the plan intends to move `_RELATION_TO_JIRA_LINK` from `outbound_links.py` into the adapter, that is a relocation, not deduplication — and relocation is explicitly out-of-scope (S5).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the concrete grep anchor(s) to AC2 — at minimum name the key symbols/constants to check for (e.g. `grep -rn 'fit_adf\|adf_size\|PRIORITY_MAP\|LINK_RELATION' rebar_reconciler/{differ,classify,...}.py`) so the proving command is deterministic.
evidence: 'Verified by grep-ing the 10 core sites for those constructs and getting zero hits'
Constructs named only in prose: 'ADF size-fitting', 'lossy status/parent mapping', 'priority/status value-maps', 'link-relation constants' — no symbol names given.
findings.finding: AC2's proving command ('grep-ing the 10 core sites for those constructs') does not specify the actual symbol or function names to grep for (e.g. the ADF size-fit function name, the lossy-status mapping constant names), leaving the executor to discover them from the codebase rather than acting on the plan.
scenarios: Executor writes a grep that misses one of the constructs (e.g. uses the wrong function name), producing a false-negative zero-hit result and shipping an incomplete rewire.
Executor's grep is overly broad, flagging false positives and causing unnecessary churn.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a second grep to AC2 that confirms the moved constructs appear in **exactly one location** repo-wide (e.g. `grep -rn 'PRIORITY_MAP' . | grep -v adapters/jira/ | grep -v test` should return zero hits) to verify single-sourcing, not just core-emptiness.
evidence: 'those constructs are invoked via the Backend mapper and defined in exactly one place'
No proving command for 'exactly one place' — only 'grep-ing the 10 core sites... getting zero hits' (proves absence in core, not uniqueness globally).
findings.finding: AC2 claims the logic is 'defined in exactly one place' (single-sourcing) but no step or command proves uniqueness — only the absence from core is verified; a second definition surviving elsewhere in the repo (e.g. a forgotten root-level copy) would not be caught.
scenarios: A duplicate definition survives at the repo root (in the old outbound_fields.py or adf.py), the AC2 grep passes because it only checks the 10 core files, and the single-source goal is silently unmet.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC3 to distinguish the two `"jira"` literals in `apply_inbound_records.py`: specify that the `RemoteRef`/`IdentityConvention` rewire targets the vendor-identity use (`ensure_identity_for` / `creation_channel=` kwarg at line 74), and explicitly state whether the `validate_creation_channel("jira")` call at line 156 is also in scope or is intentionally left as a schema-vocabulary constant.
evidence: AC3: '`apply_inbound_records.py` uses `RemoteRef`/`IdentityConvention` with no hardcoded `"jira"` literal — verified by `grep`'
apply_inbound_records.py:70: rebar.ensure_identity_for("jira", account_id, ..., creation_channel="jira")
apply_inbound_records.py:156: create_data["creation_channel"] = validate_creation_channel("jira")
The two 'jira' literals serve different contracts: vendor identity (Backend concern) vs. reducer creation-channel vocabulary (schema concern). No Backend Protocol method maps to creation_channel.
findings.finding: AC3's 'no hardcoded `"jira"` literal' grep covers two semantically distinct uses in apply_inbound_records.py: (1) line 70/74 `ensure_identity_for("jira", ...)` / `creation_channel="jira"` (identity-provider name, RemoteRef target), and (2) line 156 `validate_creation_channel("jira")` (a reducer-schema closed-vocabulary channel name, not a backend key). The plan routes only the first through `IdentityConvention`; no `Backend` method is defined to replace the `creation_channel` vocabulary string. A blanket grep for zero `"jira"` literals will either (a) still pass with one literal remaining if the author only fixes the identity site, or (b) break the creation-channel validator contract if the author removes both.
scenarios: Author fixes only the RemoteRef identity site; the creation_channel literal remains; the grep gate passes but the AC intent is ambiguous on whether it should.

Author removes both literals; validate_creation_channel receives a non-literal (e.g. backend.vendor_key()); the reducer schema validator may reject a dynamically-computed value that is not in its closed vocabulary.
findings.suggested_fix: Split AC3 into two sub-bullets: (a) 'The `ensure_identity_for` call uses `backend.identity_convention().vendor` (or equivalent `RemoteRef` field) — no bare `"jira"` string for vendor identity'; (b) 'The `validate_creation_channel("jira")` call is out of scope for this rewire (it is a reducer schema vocabulary value, not a backend key) and may retain the literal'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly address the `validate_creation_channel("jira")` literal in `apply_inbound_records.py` line 156 — either include it in the `RemoteRef`/`IdentityConvention` swap scope or note it as intentionally out of scope and adjust the AC#3 grep accordingly.
evidence: `apply_inbound_records.py` line 70: `rebar.ensure_identity_for("jira", account_id, ...)` — the identity-provider literal the plan targets.
`apply_inbound_records.py` line 156: `create_data["creation_channel"] = validate_creation_channel("jira")` — a second `"jira"` literal not addressed by the plan.

AC#3 says grep for `"jira"` must return zero hits; if only the identity-provider call is replaced, the `creation_channel` call still passes the `grep` only if `validate_creation_channel` is called with a variable — but the plan does not describe supplying a backend-derived channel value.
findings.finding: `apply_inbound_records.py` contains a second hardcoded `"jira"` literal at line 156 (`validate_creation_channel("jira")`), in addition to the `ensure_identity_for("jira", ...)` at line 70. The plan's AC#3 only mentions replacing `"jira"` with `RemoteRef`/`IdentityConvention`, which covers the identity-provider use, but does not address the `creation_channel` literal.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC#3: '`apply_inbound_records.py` uses `RemoteRef`/`IdentityConvention` with no hardcoded `"jira"` literal — verified by `grep`'
scenarios: The neutrality grep in AC#3 (`grep` for `"jira"` in the 10 sites) would still find `validate_creation_channel("jira")` after the `RemoteRef` swap, causing AC#3 to fail.
Or the implementer silently passes this literal without replacement, leaving a Jira-specific creation_channel string in core.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `rebar.ensure_identity_for("jira", ...)` provider string and `creation_channel="jira"` kwarg in `apply_inbound_records.py` (lines 74–75) are also in scope for the `RemoteRef`/`IdentityConvention` replacement, and update AC #3 to name all literal sites or explicitly exclude them with justification.
evidence: AC #3 states: 'apply_inbound_records.py uses RemoteRef/IdentityConvention with no hardcoded "jira" literal — verified by grep'
Codebase line 74: rebar.ensure_identity_for("jira", account_id, ..., creation_channel="jira")
Codebase line 156: create_data["creation_channel"] = validate_creation_channel("jira")
The provider string in rebar.ensure_identity_for is an identity-system API call, not the reconciler's own key — IdentityConvention may not cover it, and the plan does not address this distinction
findings.finding: apply_inbound_records.py contains a second 'jira' literal at line 74 (`rebar.ensure_identity_for('jira', ...)` provider string and `creation_channel='jira'`) that is structurally different from the `validate_creation_channel('jira')` at line 156. The plan's AC #3 covers the latter (via RemoteRef/IdentityConvention) but is silent on whether `ensure_identity_for`'s provider string is also replaced or left intentionally — leaving the AC gap undiscovered until implementation.
scenarios: Implementer replaces validate_creation_channel('jira') with IdentityConvention.vendor_key(), runs grep for '"jira"', and the grep still hits the ensure_identity_for call — AC #3 fails despite the intended work being done
Alternatively, implementer removes both literals without considering that ensure_identity_for's provider is a cross-system identity namespace that may legitimately stay as 'jira' even post-rewire
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC #3: 'apply_inbound_records.py uses RemoteRef/IdentityConvention for the binding key literal; the ensure_identity_for provider string ("jira") is either also replaced via backend.identity_convention().vendor_key() or explicitly excluded with a note that it is an identity-system namespace, not a reconciler key — verified by grep showing zero unintended "jira" literals.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Scope section that 'relocate into adapters/jira/' means adding new methods/logic to **existing** adapters/jira/ files (not creating new files), since physical file creation is deferred to S5 — or explicitly state which existing file receives each piece of moved logic.
evidence: 'No files are physically relocated here (that is S5); this story changes **who calls what**, not where files live.'
'Relocate the three specific leaks (ADF-sizing, lossy status/parent, duplicated maps/link constants) into adapters/jira/.'
findings.finding: The plan says 'No files are physically relocated here' but the Scope section says 'Relocate the three specific leaks... into adapters/jira/' — an executor cannot tell whether they should create new files in adapters/jira/, add methods to existing adapters/jira/ files, or only call existing adapter methods that already exist there.
findings.location: Context paragraph ('No files are physically relocated here') vs. Scope bullet ('Relocate the three specific leaks... into adapters/jira/')
scenarios: An executor adds new files to adapters/jira/ (violating the stated no-relocation constraint) when the intent was to add methods to existing adapter files.
An executor doesn't know whether to create a new FieldSanitizer method in an existing adapters/jira/ file or whether a target method already exists from S2.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical to the Scope bullet: 'Relocate the three specific leaks... into adapters/jira/ (by adding methods to existing adapter files — e.g. OutboundMapper, FieldSanitizer — not by creating new files, which is S5).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (or expand-contract sequencing) for the `RemoteRef` identity-shape change — describe how existing serialized records with the old string key are migrated or remain readable, and what the revert procedure is if the swap must be undone.
evidence: "the `RemoteRef` swap in `apply_inbound_records.py` replaces string-typed identity with the tuple from S2"
"Because every change is behavior-preserving, the existing reconcile characterization suite plus the S2 contract suite are the oracle"
No rollback path, expand-contract sequence, or data-migration step is mentioned anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The `RemoteRef`/`IdentityConvention` swap in `apply_inbound_records.py` replaces a string-typed identity with a tuple ("the tuple from S2"), which is a data-shape change, not merely a behavioral rewire. The plan asserts the change is 'behavior-preserving' and relies on the reconcile characterization suite as the rollback oracle, but names no rollback plan, no expand-contract sequence, and no migration path for any persisted or serialized records that contain the old string-typed key. If the characterization suite exercises only in-memory reconcile event equality and not serialization format, the break goes undetected until production.
findings.location: Approach ('Because every change is behavior-preserving … the tuple from S2'); Scope ('Replace the "jira" literal / bare key … with RemoteRef/IdentityConvention'); AC3
scenarios: Any serialized baseline/shadow store that holds the old string key is silently incompatible after the swap, causing reconcile to treat every record as new on the first run against an existing store.
A rollback to pre-story `main` leaves stores in the new tuple format, breaking the old code.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short 'Compatibility & Rollback' section: (1) state whether baseline/shadow stores persist the identity key; (2) if yes, provide a one-time migration script or a read-shim that accepts both the old string and new `RemoteRef` tuple during the transition window; (3) note that reverting requires re-running the inverse migration. If stores are ephemeral/rebuilt on each run, state that explicitly as the justification that no migration is needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC2's verification step to cover the replacement side: add a check (unit test or grep) that confirms the ADF size-fitting, lossy status/parent mapping, and value-map/link-relation constants ARE present and exercised in the Jira adapter after the move.
evidence: "Verified by `grep`ing the 10 core sites for those constructs and getting zero hits (the definitions now live in the Jira adapter … this AC asserts *single-sourcing + core-emptiness*, not a path)"
The AC names 'single-sourcing + core-emptiness' but only the 'core-emptiness' half has a concrete verification method.
findings.finding: AC2 is a migration criterion (remove from core + add to adapter), but its verification step describes only the removal side: 'Verified by `grep`ing the 10 core sites for those constructs and getting zero hits.' The replacement side — confirming the constructs ARE present and correctly invoked in the Jira adapter — has no stated verification step, leaving the migration half-verified.
scenarios: An implementer could delete the ADF size-fitting logic from core and forget to add it to the adapter; AC2's grep gate passes (zero core hits) but the feature is now broken — no AC catches the missing replacement.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC2: 'Replacement verified by: the Jira-adapter unit tests exercise ADF truncation at the Jira limit, lossy status/parent mapping, and priority/status value-map look-ups — all routed through `OutboundMapper`/`FieldSanitizer` on the `Backend`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify (in Scope or AC) whether the ADF size-fit threshold is a named, injectable/configurable constant in the Jira adapter rather than a magic number — and confirm tests reference that same constant, not a duplicate literal.
evidence: Plan says 'ADF size-fit currently in the differ → move behind FieldSanitizer/OutboundMapper on adapters/jira/'
Testing section says 'truncates at the Jira limit' — implying a specific numeric value
No mention of how the limit value is held (config, constant, injectable parameter)
findings.finding: The ADF size-fit threshold (Jira's content-size limit) is being moved into the adapter's FieldSanitizer/OutboundMapper, but the plan does not specify whether this threshold is injected/configurable or hardcoded as a constant inside the adapter. A hardcoded numeric limit that Jira could change (or that a non-Jira deployment might need to override) is a maintainability hazard.
findings.location: Scope section / Testing AC: 'ADF size-fitting now invoked via the mapper still truncates at the Jira limit'
scenarios: Jira raises or lowers the ADF content size limit in a future API version
A self-hosted Jira deployment has a different configured limit
A second adapter reuses FieldSanitizer base logic and needs a different threshold
findings.suggested_fix: Define a single named constant (e.g. `JIRA_ADF_CONTENT_LIMIT_BYTES`) in the adapter, reference it from both FieldSanitizer and the unit test, and note in the AC that the limit is single-sourced via this constant (not duplicated).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a task to update ADR 0035 (or the Backend port interface docstrings / AGENTS.md) with the new method contracts introduced by this story (ADF size-fit, lossy-status mapping, link-relation constant lookup) so future adapter implementors have a documented spec.
evidence: Plan references 'ADR 0035 §(a)' as the existing decision but does not propose updating it
New method contracts (ADF size-fitting, lossy status/parent) are added to the Backend port interface with no documented specification
'No files are physically relocated here' — the interface extension is the only new artifact
S2 prerequisite defined the port; this story adds new method responsibilities not present in that definition
findings.finding: The plan extends the Backend port interface (adding new responsibilities to OutboundMapper/InboundMapper/FieldSanitizer for ADF sizing and lossy status/parent mapping) but does not call for updating ADR 0035 or any interface contract document to reflect these new method signatures/contracts. A future implementor adding a second adapter has no documented spec for what these mapper methods must do.
findings.location: Context / Approach sections; AC line 2 ('The core modules no longer contain ADF size-fitting, lossy status/parent mapping…those constructs are invoked via the Backend mapper')
scenarios: A developer implements a second Backend adapter (e.g. GitHub Issues) and must guess what FieldSanitizer.fit_adf() or InboundMapper.map_lossy_status() must return
ADR 0035 is consulted as the authoritative reference but doesn't describe these new methods — reviewer approves a non-conforming implementation
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope or Testing section, add: 'Update the Backend port interface definition (abstract base class docstrings or ADR 0035 §methods) to document the new OutboundMapper.fit_adf(), InboundMapper.map_status(), and related method contracts introduced by this rewire.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add `reconcile_check.py` to the scope of sites whose duplicated priority/type value-maps are removed and replaced with Backend mapper calls, or explicitly note in Scope that reconcile_check.py's private copies are deferred (with a rationale), so the 'single-source' AC is not silently incomplete.
evidence: Scope states: 'Single-source the duplicated priority/status value-maps and link-relation constants behind the mapper (remove the core copies)'
reconcile_check.py lines 103–109: _PRIORITY_LOCAL_TO_JIRA dict literal identical to outbound_fields._LOCAL_TO_JIRA_PRIORITY and jira_fields._LOCAL_PRIORITY_TO_JIRA
reconcile_check.py lines 112–117: _TYPE_LOCAL_TO_JIRA dict literal duplicating outbound_fields._LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE
reconcile_check.py is NOT listed among the 10 differ/apply leak sites targeted by this story
findings.finding: reconcile_check.py is not in the 10-site scope list but contains its own private copies of the priority map (_PRIORITY_LOCAL_TO_JIRA, lines 103–109) and issue-type map (_TYPE_LOCAL_TO_JIRA, lines 112–117) that duplicate constants in outbound_fields.py and jira_fields.py. After the rewire, these orphaned copies in reconcile_check.py remain, leaving the 'single-source the duplicated priority/status value-maps' goal only partially achieved.
scenarios: Implementer removes value-map copies from all 10 named sites; post-story grep still finds _PRIORITY_LOCAL_TO_JIRA in reconcile_check.py — single-sourcing is incomplete and any future Jira priority rename must be updated in multiple places
The neutrality gate (checking only the 10 sites) passes green while reconcile_check.py retains duplicated Jira-specific maps

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend the existing 'make lint && make typecheck && make test' AC) that explicitly verifies no `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/*.py` (including the `adapters/jira/` destination files) exceeds the 800-LOC cap defined in `.github/module-size-limit.txt` after the S4 rewire — aligning with parent epic AC5.
evidence: Parent epic AC5: 'Every src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/*.py stays within the 800-LOC module-size cap (.github/module-size-limit.txt) throughout.'
Parent epic: 'throughout' explicitly means at every story, not only at S5.
Leaf Scope section and all five ACs contain no reference to the 800-LOC cap or .github/module-size-limit.txt.

S4 moves ADF-sizing, lossy status/parent, and duplicated maps/link-constants into adapters/jira/ mapper files — these additions could push a receiving file past the 800-LOC limit with no gate in the leaf to catch it.
findings.finding: The leaf plan omits the parent epic's 800-LOC module-size cap (AC5), which applies at every landed change including S4. S4 moves logic into adapters/jira/ mapper files that could grow substantially; no cap check is specified in the leaf's Scope, Testing, or ACs.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all five ACs) and Scope section of the leaf plan
scenarios: A developer consolidates all relocated logic into adapters/jira/jira_fields.py, pushing it past 800 LOC; the leaf's 'make lint && make typecheck && make test' gate does not catch it unless the CI size-limit check is already wired, leaving the epic's AC5 silently violated at the S4 landing boundary.
A reviewer signs off on S4 ACs without noticing the size constraint — the epic's cross-cutting cap is not re-verified until S5 discovers an oversized file.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: '- [ ] No `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/*.py` (including adapter files that receive relocated logic) exceeds the 800-LOC cap in `.github/module-size-limit.txt` after the rewire — verified by the existing size-limit CI gate or `wc -l` spot-check on the destination files in `adapters/jira/`.' Optionally note in Scope that oversized destination files should be split before or during this story.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
